Threat.value: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

Negative Appraisals.value: I have permanently changed for the worse
My reactions since the event mean that I am going crazy
I feel isolated and set apart from others

Maladaptive Strategies.value: make sure to stay close to exit
I drift off into a world of my own

vignette:
He is a 24-year-old African man in a relationship who presents with severe posttraumatic distress following sexual violence, with a PCL-5 score of 69. In day-to-day life he is frequently pulled back into the event by reminders that seem ordinary to others but feel dangerous to him, particularly the presence of police, the sound of babies crying, and shouting. When these cues appear, he becomes highly alert and tense, scanning the people around him and moving himself closer to exits. He describes this watchfulness as exhausting, yet it has become a regular part of how he gets through public spaces.

His recollections of the assault are vivid and poorly integrated, experienced more as pictures or a film that repeats than as a settled memory. When the memories surface, he notices his body taking on the same position it was in during the frightening event, and at times he feels as if it is happening again. These experiences are closely tied to intense threat sensations, with moments of flashback, difficulty falling or staying asleep, and a tendency to take unnecessary risks or do things that could cause harm. He also drifts off into a world of his own, as though trying to create distance from what is happening around him.

He speaks with a strong sense that he has permanently changed for the worse and worries that his reactions mean he is going crazy. He feels isolated and set apart from others, including his partner, and this sense of separateness weighs heavily on him. These appraisals sit at the center of his distress, colouring how he understands his symptoms and intensifying both the sense of threat and the intrusiveness of the memories.

The result is a cycle in which the reminders of the trauma, the recurring memories, and the ongoing sense of threat feed one another. His hypervigilance, staying near exits, and periods of mental escape may offer brief relief, but they leave him increasingly detached and worn down. Over time, this pattern has made it harder for him to feel safe, to sleep, and to remain emotionally present in his relationship.
